On Friday at 5:21 p.m. New York time, Antropic received a letter from the American government, and the effect of that letter was to forcibly shut down the two most powerful A models they had ever released worldwide.  Guys, it wasn't a bug, it wasn't a stability issue, it was the United States government saying: "Take it down."  Let me quickly give you some background information because there are a lot of people spreading misinformation about this on the internet.  This week Antropic released two new models, the Mitos 5 and the Fable 5. If you have n't seen the video yet, it's in my channel description; just click and watch.  Both come from the same foundation of a model called Mitos Preview, which was so strong at finding security flaws that it was only released to a closed group of companies and researchers in a group called Project Glasling.  As you can see, I'm here on the Cloud AI screen.  Look here.  Fable 5 is offline, right?  It's not working.  The difference between the two is quite simple. Myth 5 is the raw version released only to select organizations.  Fable 5 is the public version, but with security locks that block the mode in high- risk areas, like cybersecurity.  Guys, this was the first time Antropic put a model of this caliber in the hands of the general public.  And then, if you haven't tried it yet, it's over three days after launch .  What the government technically did was something called an export control directive.  In other words, it's an order prohibiting Antropic from giving access to these models to any foreigner, inside or outside the United States, including Antropic's own foreign employees.  Because of the size of this restriction, Antropic said that it was impossible to comply with it partially. To filter out foreigners, they would have to break, you know, take down half the base. So, my friend, they went there and turned it off for everyone.  If it's not allowed outside, it's not allowed inside either.  The reason, according to the government there, is that they believe someone discovered a way to bypass the Fable 5 security measures, which would be called a General Break.  The letter was signed by a Secretary of Commerce, Howard Lutnick. And that's where it gets interesting.  It's ridiculous that she didn't swallow that in silence .  She complied with the order, but released a public statement disagreeing with it on every point.  And it's important for you to understand both sides because the video that's circulating already gives you a ready-made conspiracy theory.  What did Antropic say?  First of all, this Jilak isn't universal; it's not the master key that unlocks everything.  From what they analyzed, it was basically asking the model to read code and point out its flaws, something that, according to Antropic itself, other public models already do, including Openai's GPT 5.5, which did not suffer any restrictions.  Secondly, before launching, they tested Fable for thousands of hours in conjunction with the US government, the UK's Institute for Security and Intelligence, and independent companies.  Nobody has found a universal J-break .  Third, and this is the strongest argument, they said that recalling a model used by hundreds of millions of people because of a narrow flaw sets a dangerous precedent.  If that standard applied to everyone, practically no new model could be launched by anyone. In summary, their position is that the government can indeed have the power to block unsafe launches, but within a clear and transparent process based on technical facts.  And in Antropic's view, that's not what happened here.  Tell, a well-known creator in the developer community, posted here on the forum, saying that the three days using the Fable model were, without a doubt, the best, unlike anything he had ever used before, and that it was too good to be true, but the government ends up interfering.  And it wasn't just empty hype; the model delivered.  a guy named Todd Saounder.  He explained that he was in a call with the client, with the cloud transcribing in the background, while the client described the functions he wanted.  The model was being built in real time, and by the end of the call, 15 minutes later, it had a working product with the exact flow the client had just described.  This is the level of tool that went offline. Now I'm going to tell you something that I really think we shouldn't be talking about without knowing exactly what we're talking about.  Some people are saying that this was Antropic's plan from the beginning, that they launched the model, got everyone hooked, and used the government as an excuse to cut funding because running these models is too expensive. That's a theory, but the facts available today point to the contrary.  Antropic publicly disagreed, apologized to its customers, and is working to restore access as quickly as possible.  Hey guys, the company that stages its own charade doesn't usually publicly fight with those who should be its accomplices. More information might come out and change everything, but so far it's just speculation, so we're not going to say anything stupid in videos or any other channel. But look, in practice, for the average user, Fable 5 and Myths 5 would be taken offline.  Opus 4.8, Sony, and Haiku continue to function normally.  So if you use the cloud to build solutions, applications, and automations on a daily basis, everything is normal.  But what happened here is bigger than the model. For the first time, a giant AI company was pulled from the plow because of direct intervention from the federal government. This is undoubtedly a milestone.  And it puts a question on the table that's worth more than any benchmark.  To what extent will access to the most powerful tools remain yours, and when will the decision rest with the government?  Now, what I think about this, as someone who actually uses these tools for building, is that almost no one is discussing the difference between a narrow jilbreak and a universal jilbreak.  Antropic didn't build Fable, thinking it would be impossible to cheat.  They assumed that a point-in-time Jbreak would exist and built a layered defense , including locks, weaker redirection for sensitive data, and a 30-day data retention period to monitor attacks.  This is a gamble and a solid engineering effort, not carelessness.  And the ability that the government cited—to read code and point out flaws—is something that GPT 5.5 and other public models already do, and that professionals use every single day to defend the system. So, guys, what's worrying me is n't that Fable is coming out, what worries me is the standard.  Recalling a model from the hands of hundreds of millions of people because of a flaw that exists across the entire market, without clear technical evidence , is exactly the kind of opaque process that stifles innovation.  And irony is good.  The company Antropic is the one that has requested regulation the most, but it has requested transparent regulation based on facts.  And what she got was the opposite of that.  For me, in the end, what matters isn't even that the model was released.  The model comes and goes all the time.  What matters is the precedent. Today was Fable.  The rule that decided this will still apply to future cases.  I will continue to follow along .  If they restore access, I'll make a video. I'll continue to monitor the situation here, but I want you to leave your thoughts in the comments .  Is this true national security or just excessive control?
